_meta.description: Orlog PM Personality Test — Balanced Question Bank v3
_meta.balanceNote: Each type has exactly ~200 points available across the full bank. Sliders are distributed exactly 4 per type (2 as high_maps_to, 2 as low_maps_to). Secondary +1 points are spread evenly ~67 per type.

dimensions.anchor: The Sovereign — High ownership, independent, founder-minded

_meta.scoringNote: Each MC option: +2 primary, +1 secondary. Sliders: score 4-5 = high_maps_to gets +2, score 1-2 = low_maps_to gets +2, score 3 = neutral (+0).
_meta.storageStrategy: JSON file in /constants/ — static, bundled at build time. Supabase used only for session writes and admin reads.

questions.text: Your company is planning next year's roadmap. The CEO wants a 3-year vision. Your engineering lead wants a 6-month delivery plan. You have one week. What do you focus on?

options.text: Build a compelling 3-year vision narrative with market trends and opportunity sizing.
Create a detailed 6-month delivery plan with milestones, owners, and dependencies.
Talk to customers first to validate what problems are worth solving before committing.
Facilitate a cross-functional session to align both the CEO and engineering on a shared plan.

questions.text: A competitor just launched a feature your team has been debating for 6 months. Leadership is panicking. What is your first move?

options.text: Zoom out — assess whether this competitor move changes your long-term strategic bet at all.
Immediately scope the fastest version of the feature your team can ship in 4 weeks.
Call 10 customers to understand if this competitor feature actually matters to them.
Pull together leadership, engineering, and design to align on a response before acting.

questions.text: You are given 20% time with no deliverables attached. How do you use it?

options.text: Research emerging trends and adjacent markets to identify your next big bet.
Clear the backlog of small improvements your team has been too busy to ship.
Spend it entirely on customer interviews and unmoderated user research.
Run structured experiments on hypotheses you've been meaning to test.

questions.text: You are writing a product strategy document. Which section do you spend the most time on?

options.text: The vision and market opportunity — painting a picture of where the world is going.
The execution plan — phases, timelines, success metrics, and owners.
The user problem definition — making sure the pain point is real and well-understood.
The stakeholder alignment section — who needs to be bought in and how.

questions.text: Your product is growing slowly. The board wants to 10x growth in 18 months. What is your instinctive response?

options.text: Identify a bold strategic pivot or new market expansion that could unlock exponential growth.
Map every current growth lever, find the top 3, and execute on them with relentless focus.
Go deep with your best customers to understand what would make them refer others.
Convene a cross-functional growth task force with marketing, sales, and engineering.

questions.text: Feature A takes 3 months with guaranteed moderate impact. Feature B takes 6 months with a small chance of massive impact. Which do you fight for?

options.text: Feature B — the asymmetric upside is worth the risk and longer timeline.
Feature A — predictable impact shipped faster is more valuable than a moonshot.
Neither until I've validated both with real users to see which problem is more painful.
Present both options to stakeholders and build consensus before deciding.

questions.text: A senior engineer says your 1-year roadmap is unrealistic — it's 2 years of work. How do you respond?

options.text: Stand firm on the vision — ambitious timelines push teams to think creatively and cut scope.
Immediately sit down with them to re-scope the roadmap into realistic phases.
Ask which parts of the roadmap will most directly impact user outcomes.
Make the call yourself — you have the context and conviction to decide.

questions.text: You are onboarding to a new PM role. In your first 30 days, what are you primarily focused on?

options.text: Understanding the market landscape, competitive dynamics, and long-term strategic direction.
Learning the current sprint, backlog, and what is blocking the team from shipping.
Speaking to as many customers as possible to understand their problems firsthand.
Building relationships with every stakeholder across engineering, design, sales, and leadership.

questions.text: You can own Product X — early-stage 0-to-1 with no clear path — or Product Y — mature with clear metrics. Which do you choose?

options.text: Product X — building something from nothing with freedom to define the vision is energizing.
Product Y — clear metrics, known roadmap, and defined success criteria let me execute well.
Product X — early stage means more direct customer contact and real problem discovery.
Product Y — a mature product means a larger team and more cross-functional collaboration.

questions.text: Your product is at a major inflection point. Double down on current strategy or pivot to a new market. How do you decide?

options.text: Trust your conviction — you have a point of view on where the market is going.
Run a structured analysis: compare ROI, risk, and timeline of both paths.
Talk to your best and worst customers — their answers will tell you whether to stay or pivot.
Convene a strategy offsite with all key stakeholders to debate and align.

questions.text: A junior PM asks you what makes a great product. What is your answer?

options.text: A great product solves a future problem before the user knows they have it.
A great product ships reliably, works well, and continuously improves based on feedback.
A great product makes a user's life meaningfully better in a way they can feel.
A great product is built by a team that is deeply aligned and trusted by the whole company.

questions.text: You are presenting your product area to the board. What does your presentation lead with?

options.text: The market opportunity and your 3-year vision for where this product category is heading.
What you shipped last quarter, what you're shipping next, and key delivery metrics.
Customer stories and evidence that your product is solving real, painful problems.
The data behind every decision — metrics, experiments, and what you learned.

questions.text: Your team's headcount is being cut by 30% next quarter. What is your immediate priority?

options.text: Redefine what your product needs to become to stay relevant with a smaller team.
Immediately audit the roadmap and cut everything that isn't essential.
Identify which customer commitments must be protected and ensure those aren't compromised.
Have honest conversations with the team and leadership to align on the full impact.

questions.text: A new technology just became accessible to your industry. What is your reaction?

options.text: Immediately start exploring how this technology could fundamentally change your product category.
Wait for the technology to mature, then adopt it when there is a clear use case with proven ROI.
Ask users whether they would actually want this technology in the product before exploring it.
Bring engineering and design together to evaluate feasibility and form a shared point of view.

questions.text: Your team just shipped a major feature. It performs okay — not great, not bad. What is your next step?

options.text: Already thinking about version 2.0 — what this feature could become in 12 months.
Run a tight post-launch retro, document learnings, and optimize the feature iteratively.
Call users who used the feature and those who didn't — understand the gap.
Dig into the data — funnel drop-offs, engagement rates, cohort behavior.

questions.text: When writing a product spec, you naturally spend the most time on:

options.text: The 'Why Now' section — market timing, strategic context, opportunity framing.
The requirements and edge cases — making sure engineering has everything they need.
The user stories and jobs-to-be-done — keeping it grounded in real human behavior.
The metrics and success criteria — how will we know if this worked?

questions.text: You are most energized when:

options.text: Crafting a product strategy that could change the direction of the company.
Watching a feature you planned carefully go live without a single bug.
Sitting with a user and watching them have an 'aha' moment with your product.
Proving a hypothesis right with clean data from a well-designed experiment.

questions.text: You are most frustrated when:

options.text: Your team is too focused on short-term tasks to think about where the product needs to go.
Deadlines are missed or plans change without clear reason.
Decisions are made without talking to a single customer.
Teams work in silos and nobody communicates what they are doing.

questions.text: Your ideal product review meeting is one where:

options.text: The team debates bold strategic bets and challenges assumptions about the future.
Every item has a clear owner, deadline, and status — no ambiguity.
Real user feedback and research is at the center of every decision.
Every function — engineering, design, marketing — has a voice and feels heard.

questions.text: When you think about your product 3 years from now, you are most focused on:

options.text: The category you will have defined or disrupted.
The systems, processes, and infrastructure you will have built.
The number of users whose lives you will have meaningfully improved.
The cross-functional culture and team you will have shaped.

questions.text: You get a 1-hour slot with your CEO. You use it to discuss:

options.text: Where the market is heading and what strategic bets the company should be making now.
What is blocking the team from shipping faster and what needs to change.
What you are hearing from customers and what it means for the product direction.
How to improve cross-functional collaboration and reduce friction between teams.

questions.text: If you had to describe your product thinking style in one word, it would be:

options.text: Visionary
Systematic
Empathetic
Analytical

questions.text: I am more excited by defining where a product should go than by ensuring it gets there on time.
I find it hard to stay motivated when working on incremental improvements rather than transformational ideas.
A well-structured delivery plan gives me more confidence than a compelling vision document.
You run an A/B test on a new onboarding flow. Results are inconclusive after 3 weeks. Your gut says the new flow is better. What do you do?

options.text: Wait longer for the data to reach significance before making any decision.
Ship the new flow — your intuition combined with directional data is enough to act.
Talk to users who went through both flows to understand their qualitative experience.
Bring the team together to review the data collectively and decide as a group.

questions.text: A sales rep tells you that 5 enterprise clients want a specific feature. You have no other data. Do you build it?

options.text: No — 5 data points is not enough. Run a proper discovery process before committing resources.
Yes — enterprise signal from sales is real signal. Trust it and move quickly.
Maybe — but only after interviewing those 5 clients directly to understand the real need.
Bring sales and engineering together to assess feasibility and strategic fit before deciding.

questions.text: Your metrics look healthy — retention is up, NPS is high. But something feels off about the product direction. What do you do?

options.text: Trust the metrics. If numbers are healthy, the feeling is probably noise — stay the course.
Trust the feeling. Metrics are lagging indicators. Dig into what's causing your instinct.
Go talk to users immediately — your instinct might be picking up something they're experiencing.
Share the feeling with the team and see if others are picking up on the same signal.

questions.text: You have strong quantitative data supporting Feature A and a strong personal conviction about Feature B. Which do you choose?

options.text: Feature A — data-backed decisions are more defensible and less risky.
Feature B — you've seen enough to know when data doesn't capture the full picture.
Neither until you validate both with customers to see which problem is more acutely felt.
Present both to the team with your reasoning and make the call together.

questions.text: You are asked to make a product decision on the spot in a leadership meeting without time to review data.

options.text: Ask for 24 hours to pull the relevant data before committing to a direction.
Make the call confidently based on your knowledge and experience.
Share what you've heard from customers as the basis for your on-the-spot recommendation.
Open the decision to the room — use collective intelligence rather than a solo call.

questions.text: Research shows users say they want Feature X. But your experience tells you what users say and what they do are very different.

options.text: Run a behavioral experiment — measure what users actually do, not what they say.
Trust your experience. You know how to read between the lines of user feedback.
Do deeper qualitative research — observe users in context to see the real behavior.
Share the tension with the team and debate it collectively before making a call.

questions.text: You have 3 months of strong growth data. A senior leader suggests it might be a seasonal spike, not real PMF.

options.text: Dig into the data — cohort analysis, retention curves, and channel breakdowns will reveal the truth.
Disagree with conviction — you've been watching user behavior closely and this feels real.
Talk to new users from this period to understand why they came and why they stayed.
Propose a framework the whole leadership team can use to evaluate it together.

questions.text: You are about to kill a feature based on low usage. One passionate power user says removing it would make them churn.

options.text: Stick to the data — one user's preference cannot override aggregate usage signals.
Pause and investigate — a passionate user like this often represents an undervalued segment.
Talk to them in depth — understand exactly how and why they use this feature before deciding.
Share the signal with the team — get multiple perspectives before making the final call.

questions.text: You are evaluating whether to enter a new market. You have incomplete data but a strong strategic conviction.

options.text: Commission proper market research before committing any resources.
Run a lean experiment in the new market with a small team and minimal investment.
Talk to potential users in the new market before making any decision.
Build a cross-functional business case and evaluate it collectively with leadership.

questions.text: Your team is split 50/50 on which direction to take the product. Data supports both sides slightly. You need to break the deadlock.

options.text: Design a definitive experiment that will produce a clear data signal to resolve the tie.
Make the call yourself — someone has to decide, and you have enough context to choose.
Take both directions to users and let their reaction guide the decision.
Facilitate a structured debate until the team converges — forced decisions without buy-in don't stick.

questions.text: You are building a brand new product category where no benchmarks exist. How do you set your success metrics?

options.text: Define proxy metrics based on adjacent categories and refine them as you learn more.
Set bold, intuition-driven targets and adjust them based on what you observe after launch.
Define success through customer outcomes — if users are better off, you're succeeding.
Co-create metrics with your team and stakeholders so everyone has ownership.

questions.text: An influential stakeholder pushes back on your product decision with opinions but no data.

options.text: Respond with your own data — frame the conversation around evidence and move it there.
Hold your position — you have conviction and won't be moved without good reason.
Propose going to users together to let customer reality settle the disagreement.
Find a way to incorporate their perspective — maintaining the relationship matters.

questions.text: You get early access to a competitor's product before it launches. It looks impressive. How does this change your decision-making?

options.text: Quantify specifically how it compares to yours on key metrics before drawing conclusions.
Trust your read — you know your market well enough to assess threat level quickly.
Show it to your best customers and ask them directly what they think.
Bring it to the team for a collective threat assessment before deciding how to respond.

questions.text: A data analyst presents findings that contradict your product thesis. The methodology seems sound.

options.text: Accept the findings and update your thesis — good data should always change your mind.
Probe the findings carefully — data can be technically correct but strategically misleading.
Pair the quantitative findings with qualitative user interviews before drawing conclusions.
Share the findings with the broader team and work through the implications together.

questions.text: You need to make a pricing decision but your market data is 18 months old.

options.text: Commission fresh pricing research before making any changes — outdated data is unreliable.
Use your judgment, set a price, and treat it as an experiment you can adjust quickly.
Talk to current customers about what they value and what price feels fair.
Bring sales, finance, and marketing into the room — pricing is a cross-functional decision.

questions.text: When a decision must be made and data is limited, you:

options.text: Wait and gather more data even if it delays the decision.
Make the call and own the outcome — inaction is the worst option.
Quickly run a lightweight user test to get directional signal.
Pull the team together and make a collective judgment call.

questions.text: You trust most in:

options.text: Statistically significant data from well-designed experiments.
Your pattern recognition built from years of experience.
What users tell you and show you when you observe them closely.
The collective intelligence of a well-aligned, experienced team.

questions.text: The phrase that best describes your decision-making is:

options.text: 'Show me the data.'
'I know what needs to be done.'
'Let's ask a user.'
'What does the team think?'

questions.text: Your biggest risk as a PM is probably:

options.text: Analysis paralysis — spending too long seeking certainty before acting.
Overconfidence — trusting your gut when the data says otherwise.
Over-indexing on individual user feedback at the expense of aggregate signals.
Consensus-seeking — taking too long to get buy-in before moving.

questions.text: The metric you track most obsessively is:

options.text: Retention or engagement cohorts — they tell the real story of product health.
Revenue or growth — those prove the product is truly working.
NPS or CSAT — customer sentiment is the leading indicator I care about most.
Team velocity and shipping cadence — healthy teams build healthy products.

questions.text: When onboarding to a new product area, the first thing you do is:

options.text: Audit all available data — funnels, retention, engagement, support tickets.
Form your own hypothesis about what is broken before looking at anyone else's opinions.
Schedule 10 user interviews in the first 2 weeks.
Map all the stakeholders and understand their perspectives on the product.

questions.text: When you are wrong about a product decision, it is usually because:

options.text: I over-relied on data that didn't capture the full human context.
I was too confident and didn't seek enough outside perspective.
I listened too closely to vocal users and missed the silent majority.
I prioritized harmony and didn't push back hard enough on a flawed consensus.

questions.text: I feel uncomfortable making product decisions without statistically significant data to support them.
I have made product calls that went against the data and turned out to be correct.
Talking to users is more valuable than analysing dashboards when forming a product opinion.
You strongly disagree with a product decision that has already been made by leadership. What do you do?

options.text: Raise your concern clearly with data and reasoning, then commit fully once the decision stands.
Push back hard and continue escalating until you are heard — bad decisions need reversing.
Understand the user impact and use that to reframe your concern more effectively.
Find common ground — understand leadership's reasoning and try to synthesize both perspectives.

questions.text: You need a decision from a partner team but they are unresponsive and blocking your progress.

options.text: Document the blocker, escalate it through the right channels with data on the impact.
Make the decision yourself and move forward — you can't let others slow you down.
Understand why they are unresponsive — maybe there's a user concern you can address.
Invest time in the relationship — get on a call and work through it together.

questions.text: Your engineering lead proposes a technical approach you think is wrong. How do you handle it?

options.text: Bring data and specific examples to the conversation to make your case clearly.
Push back directly and make your position clear — you have an informed view.
Ask how the approach will affect the user experience before taking a position.
Facilitate a discussion between engineering and design to explore alternatives together.

questions.text: You have a 2-week window to explore a new product opportunity with no guidance. How do you structure your time?

options.text: Define a clear hypothesis, design experiments to test it, and analyze results.
Work independently and intensely — you produce your best thinking without external input.
Spend most of the time with users — the opportunity will emerge from listening.
Bring together a small cross-functional group and explore the opportunity collaboratively.

questions.text: A critical feature needs to ship in 3 days. You can work through the weekend alone or wait to loop in stakeholders on Monday.

options.text: Work through the weekend with clear documentation so stakeholders can review async.
Ship it — speed matters more than perfect alignment, and you'll deal with feedback after.
Assess user impact — if users are negatively affected by waiting, ship solo. If not, wait.
Do a quick 30-minute async update to key stakeholders on Friday and get fast approval.

questions.text: Your product requires input from 5 teams to move forward. Two of them are slow to respond.

options.text: Map the dependencies, identify which are truly blocking, and only escalate those.
Scope around the slow teams — find a version of the solution that doesn't need them.
Understand what would make these teams more responsive — maybe there's a shared user goal.
Invest in building a better working relationship with both teams before the next dependency.

questions.text: You are leading a product planning session and the team cannot reach a decision. What do you do?

options.text: Propose using a clear decision framework and work through it together systematically.
Make the call as the DRI — that's why you're in the room. Decide and move on.
Redirect to the user — frame every option around 'which one serves our users better?'
Keep facilitating until the group genuinely aligns — a forced decision won't stick.

questions.text: You are given ownership of a new product area with a team of 8 people you've never worked with.

options.text: Review all existing data, docs, and metrics before meeting anyone.
Quickly establish your working style, expectations, and decision-making authority.
Start with user research — understand the customer before anything else.
Have 1:1s with every team member to understand their perspectives and build trust.

questions.text: You believe a feature should be cut. Your design lead has invested 3 weeks in it and is emotionally attached.

options.text: Show data that supports cutting it and make the case calmly and clearly.
Make the call — a PM who avoids hard decisions out of emotional sensitivity isn't serving the product.
Anchor the conversation on the user — frame it as 'what is best for the person using this?'
Have a private conversation with the design lead first to find shared ground.

questions.text: You join a company where everything requires 6 approvals. How do you adapt?

options.text: Map the approval process, find bottlenecks, and propose improvements with evidence.
Find the edges of your autonomy and operate at maximum independence within them.
Use customer outcomes as the shared language that speeds up alignment across approvers.
Build relationships with all 6 approvers so the process becomes faster naturally.

questions.text: A cross-functional partner openly disagrees with your roadmap in a public meeting.

options.text: Acknowledge their point, propose discussing it offline with data in hand, and move on.
Defend your position clearly and confidently — public challenges deserve public responses.
Redirect to user impact — frame the disagreement around what matters most to customers.
Thank them for raising it, invite their input, and commit to following up together.

questions.text: You are most productive when:

options.text: You have clear goals, clean data, and time to think without interruptions.
You have full ownership and the freedom to move fast without approval chains.
You are deeply embedded with users and can test ideas quickly with them.
You are working in a high-trust team where everyone is aligned and communicating openly.

questions.text: A new PM asks you what skill they should prioritize developing.

options.text: Learn to think in data — build a quantitative intuition for what metrics actually mean.
Develop ownership — act like a founder, not a feature manager.
Get close to users — the best PMs understand people most deeply.
Learn to align people — building trust across functions is how things get done.

questions.text: Your company introduces a policy limiting your autonomy — more approvals are now required.

options.text: Adapt — build data practices that make your approvals faster and more defensible.
Push back — excess process is a tax on shipping, and you'll advocate for removing it.
Assess whether the policy protects or harms the user experience, then form a view.
Try to understand why the policy was introduced — maybe it's solving a real problem.

questions.text: You are presenting a roadmap to 10 senior stakeholders with very different priorities.

options.text: Build a data-backed narrative that objectively shows why each roadmap item was chosen.
Present your conviction clearly — you've done the work, you know what's right.
Anchor every roadmap item to a specific user problem — let user needs be the common ground.
Pre-socialize the roadmap with each stakeholder individually before the big meeting.

questions.text: Your ideal working environment is:

options.text: Structured, data-rich, with clear goals and measurable outputs.
Autonomous, fast-paced, with minimal bureaucracy and maximum ownership.
User-centric, with constant access to customers and rapid feedback loops.
Collaborative, high-trust, with open communication and shared ownership.

questions.text: The thing that slows you down most as a PM is:

options.text: Not having the right data to make confident decisions.
Needing too many approvals before you can move.
Not having enough access to real users.
Misalignment between teams that creates rework.

questions.text: When you ship a product that fails, your first instinct is to:

options.text: Analyze the data thoroughly to understand exactly what went wrong.
Own it fully, course-correct fast, and move forward without dwelling.
Go back to users immediately to understand how they experienced the failure.
Run a team retrospective so everyone learns and stays aligned.

questions.text: The PM superpower you are most proud of is:

options.text: Turning ambiguous problems into clear, measurable hypotheses.
Making hard calls quickly and owning the outcome completely.
Building a deep, almost uncanny understanding of your users.
Making every team feel like the product is theirs too.

questions.text: On a Friday afternoon with no meetings, you instinctively spend your time:

options.text: Reviewing dashboards, reading research, and building your knowledge base.
Moving something forward that has been blocked all week — solo, uninterrupted.
Scheduling user interviews for next week or reviewing recorded sessions.
Catching up with teammates to strengthen relationships and check on morale.

questions.text: The meeting format you find most valuable is:

options.text: A focused data review with clear takeaways and next actions.
A working session where decisions get made on the spot.
A customer listening session or user research share-out.
A cross-functional sync that builds shared understanding and alignment.

questions.text: Your team is performing well. What do you credit most?

options.text: Clear goals, good instrumentation, and disciplined prioritization.
Strong ownership culture — everyone acts like a founder of their area.
Deep customer empathy baked into every decision the team makes.
Psychological safety and open communication across all functions.

questions.text: I prefer to make important product decisions independently rather than seeking team consensus first.
I find deep satisfaction in building alignment across teams, even when it takes longer than deciding alone.
I am energized by having full ownership and accountability over a product, even if it means carrying more risk.
One sprint left in the quarter. You can ship a feature that delights users but has no direct revenue impact, or one that increases revenue 8% but users find slightly annoying. What do you ship?

options.text: The user delight feature — long-term retention is worth more than short-term revenue.
The revenue feature — an 8% lift is a concrete, defensible business outcome.
Neither — go back and find a solution that achieves both, even if it takes longer.
Consult the team and key stakeholders — this is a values call that shouldn't be made alone.

questions.text: Your NPS drops by 10 points in one month but all business metrics remain flat. What do you do?

options.text: Treat it as a critical signal — NPS drops are a leading indicator of churn and you act immediately.
Monitor it but don't panic — business metrics are stable, so this may be noise.
Dig into the data — find the segment where NPS dropped and understand the cause.
Share it transparently with the team and decide together how much weight to give it.

questions.text: A user writes an emotional support ticket saying your product change made their daily work significantly harder.

options.text: Personally read the ticket and immediately flag it internally — one user's pain is a signal.
Route it to support and check the aggregate support volume data before escalating.
Use it as an input to re-evaluate the change's broader strategic impact.
Escalate it to the relevant team and ensure someone owns the response.

questions.text: A product decision will increase DAU by 15% but the experience feels manipulative to you. Do you ship it?

options.text: No — user trust is more valuable than any short-term metric gain.
Yes — 15% DAU growth is a significant business outcome.
Redesign it — there's a way to get the metric impact without the manipulative feel.
Bring it to leadership for a values discussion — this decision shouldn't sit with one person.

questions.text: Power users drive 80% of revenue. Casual users make up 80% of volume. Your roadmap must prioritize one. Which do you choose?

options.text: Casual users — the larger segment is where long-term growth potential lies.
Power users — they drive the revenue that keeps the product alive.
Find the overlap — what would serve both segments and generate outsized returns?
Align with leadership and sales on which segment is strategically more important.

questions.text: Finance asks you to add a feature that will make monetization more aggressive but will frustrate users.

options.text: Push back with user data showing the long-term risk to retention and LTV.
Find a monetization approach that meets the revenue goal with minimal user friction.
Propose a bigger, bolder monetization strategy that doesn't require compromising the experience.
Bring finance, marketing, and product together to find a solution everyone can live with.

questions.text: You are defining success metrics for a new feature. Which metric do you weight most heavily?

options.text: User satisfaction score (CSAT/NPS) — the user's experience is the ultimate signal.
Revenue or conversion impact — that's the metric the business actually cares about.
Strategic positioning — does it help us win the market or build a defensible advantage?
Engagement depth — how much time and value are users extracting from the feature?

questions.text: Churn spikes suddenly. You can investigate either a UX issue or a pricing issue this week. Which do you choose?

options.text: UX issue — if users are struggling with the product, that's the most urgent problem.
Pricing — if pricing is wrong, no UX improvement will stop the churn.
Run a lightweight study that tests both hypotheses simultaneously.
Ask the team — someone else may have already seen signal on one of these.

questions.text: A segment that loves your product has low monetization potential. A high-monetization segment finds your product just okay. Where do you focus?

options.text: Double down on the segment that loves it — build on that love to unlock referral power.
Focus on the high monetization segment — love doesn't pay the bills.
Find product changes that would make the high-monetization segment love it too.
Align with leadership on company strategy before committing to one segment.

questions.text: Activation rate is low. You can simplify onboarding (better UX) or add a paywall earlier (better business). Which do you prioritize?

options.text: Simplify onboarding — users who don't activate never become paying customers anyway.
Add the paywall — it filters for serious users and improves the business model.
Redesign the entire onboarding to deliver value so fast the paywall feels natural.
Run both as parallel experiments and let the data decide.

questions.text: A feature your team is proud of is used by only 3% of users despite being prominently placed.

options.text: Talk to the 3% who use it — understand if it's deeply valuable to a critical segment.
Kill or deprioritize it — 3% usage is a resource drain.
Investigate whether the feature is solving the right problem — maybe it needs to be reimagined.
Present the data to the team and make the cut/keep decision together.

questions.text: You are writing your annual product strategy. The first principle you write is:

options.text: 'Users first — every decision starts with understanding the human problem behind it.'
'Business outcomes are the measure of our success — we build what moves the numbers.'
'We build for the future — we solve problems users don't know they have yet.'
'We win together — great products are built by aligned, empowered teams.'

questions.text: The executive team wants a feature users haven't asked for but with strong revenue potential.

options.text: Validate it with users first — if they don't need it, the revenue potential is speculative.
Build a proper business case and if the numbers hold, ship it.
Explore whether there is a version that creates genuine user value and drives revenue.
Facilitate a structured discussion with the executive team to align on priorities.

questions.text: Your product has a power user who generates 50% of support tickets but is also your most engaged segment.

options.text: Invest heavily in them — engaged users who push you hard are your best product partners.
Assess their business value carefully — support cost must be weighed against LTV.
Use their feedback to imagine what the product needs to become — they're showing you the future.
Bring support, success, and product together to build a shared strategy for this segment.

questions.text: You choose between two OKRs: 'Improve user satisfaction by 15%' vs 'Increase revenue per user by 20%'. Which do you fight for?

options.text: User satisfaction — it is the driver of everything else, including revenue.
Revenue per user — that demonstrates real product-market fit and business health.
Reframe both into a single OKR that captures the relationship between satisfaction and revenue.
Align with the leadership team on which one matters most to the company right now.

questions.text: The question you ask most often in a product review is:

options.text: 'How does this make the user's life better?'
'What is the expected business impact?'
'Does this move us closer to our long-term vision?'
'Does everyone on the team understand why we're building this?'

questions.text: The product failure that would upset you most is:

options.text: Shipping something that made users' lives worse, even if it hit business metrics.
Shipping something beloved by users but that destroyed the business model.
Shipping something tactically successful but strategically irrelevant.
Shipping something that fractured the team or broke cross-functional trust.

questions.text: When you read a product case study, you pay most attention to:

options.text: How the team understood and empathized with their users.
The business model decisions and how revenue grew.
The strategic bets that defined the product's long-term direction.
How the team collaborated and what their culture was like.

questions.text: If your product gets a glowing user review but misses its revenue target, you feel:

options.text: Proud — user love is the foundation. Revenue will follow.
Concerned — a missed revenue target is a missed product target, regardless of sentiment.
Curious — something in the strategy is misaligned and you want to find and fix it.
Mixed — you want to understand how the team feels and align on what to do next.

questions.text: Your product is described by users as 'essential' but your CFO calls it 'undermonetized'. Who do you agree with more?

options.text: 'Essential' is the highest compliment a product can receive — protect it above all else.
'Undermonetized' is a real problem — essential products that don't capture value won't survive.
Both are true and together they point to a massive strategic opportunity.
This tension needs a leadership conversation — it reflects a values misalignment in the company.

questions.text: You would describe your relationship with users as:

options.text: Their advocate — I represent them in every room they are not in.
Their value provider — I measure success by the outcomes they help us achieve.
Their guide — I show them possibilities they didn't know existed.
Their collaborator — the best products are built with users, not just for them.

questions.text: The PM book or resource you'd recommend to everyone is most likely about:

options.text: Customer empathy, user research, and understanding human behavior.
Business strategy, growth, and metrics-driven product development.
Vision, innovation, and building category-defining products.
Team dynamics, influence without authority, and cross-functional leadership.

questions.text: I believe that building something users genuinely love always leads to better business outcomes in the long run.
When a product decision comes down to user experience vs. business metrics, I default to the one that moves the business metrics.
I feel more accountable to my users than to my company's revenue targets.
